revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nezu Shrine
根津神社 | image = Nezu Shrine 2010.jpg | caption = The Nezu Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|43|13|N|139|45|39|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Yamato Takeru]] | established = 1705 | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-28-9 Nezu, [[Bunkyō]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 113-0031 | website = {{URL|http://www.nedujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nezu Shrine'''|根津神社|Nezu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Bunkyō]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established in 1705, it is one of the oldest places of worship in the city, and several of the buildings on the shrine grounds have been designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. It was built in the [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] style of [[Shinto architecture]], following the [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū shrine]] in [[Nikkō]]. It is famous{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} for its [[Azalea]] Festival (Tsutsuji Matsuri) which is held on its grounds from early April until early May, and it has been described as "[[Tokyo]]’s most beautiful shrine"{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and as one of the city's "most spectacular spring scenes".{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}.{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == History == According to the legend, the Nezu shrine was founded in Sendagi, just north of the current location, in the 1st century by [[Yamato Takeru]] (also known as Prince Ōsu), the son of [[Emperor Keikō]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Shinto-Shrines-Sacred-Ancient-Religion/dp/0824837134 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |pages=81 |language=English}} The chief deity of the shrine was [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the [[kami]] of the [[sea]] and [[storms]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In 1705{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo's most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} the shrine was relocated to Nezu by [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1646–1709), the fifth ''[[shōgun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa dynasty]], on the occasion of him choosing his successor, [[Tokugawa Ienobu]] (1662–1712).{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In turn, [[Ienobu]] chose it as the guardian deity.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Nothing is left from the structures prior to the relocation,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} but it still is one of the oldest shrines in the city.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} When [[Emperor Meiji]] moved his residence from the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] to the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] in [[Tokyo]] in 1868-1869, he sent envoys to the shrine to have it intercede with the gods on his behalf.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == Architecture == === Torii === There is a multitude of {{nihongo|''[[torii]]''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} surrounding Nezu Shrine. The two main entrances are marked by big red ''torii'' in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A plaque on top of them reads ''根津神社'', the name of the shrine. They are flanked by lanterns. One of the most famous{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} features of the shrine is the path of vermilion torii through the hillside left of the main hall. In the middle of the path there is a viewing platform over a pond of [[koi]], overlooking the main shrine precincts. The subsidiary Otome Inari Shrine is located here.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Another shorter path of torii leads down some stairs from the subsidiary Komagome Shrine to the larger path of torii. File:Nezu jinja - Torii 1.jpg|red ''myōjin torii'' at one of the entrances File:Nezu jinja - Torii 4.jpg|a stone ''myōjin torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 3.jpg|entrance to the path of ''torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 2.jpg|a shorter ''torii'' path === Rōmon === A two-storied {{nihongo|''[[rōmon]]''|楼門||tower gate}} is a type of [[Mon (architecture)|mon]] characterized by an inaccessible upper floor. Originally an element of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], this type of mon is also commonly found in [[Shinto shrine]]s. The one at Nezu shrine is located between the ''honden'' and a little bridge, which makes it a popular attraction of the shrine.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A placque on top of the gate reads {{nihongo|''Nezu-jinja''|根津神社}}. It is guarded by two [[Zuijin|zuishin]],{{cite web |url=http://www.visiting-japan.com/en/articles/tokyo/e13bu-nezu-jinja.htm |title=Nezu Shrine, Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Visiting Japan |access-date=2017-12-24 |quote=}} statues of [[kami]] warrior-guardians depicted holding bows and arrows. It was built in 1706,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and it is designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Romon 2.jpg|front view of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 3.jpg|placque with the name of the shrine File:Nezu jinja - Romon 1.jpg|detail of one side of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 5.jpg|one of the two ''[[Zuijin|zuishin]]'' statue === Honden === The main building of Nezu shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof. The whole structure dates from 1706. All three parts of the building (''honden'', ''haiden'' and ''heiden'') are separately designated as an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Honden 12.jpg|front view of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 4.jpg|detail of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 11.jpg|interior of the ''honden'' and ''haiden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 1.jpg|the ''honden'' seen from the viewing platform === Karamon and Sukibei walls === A {{nihongo|''[[karamon]]''|唐門|"chinese gate"}} is a type of ''mon'' found in [[Japanese castle]]s, [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine]]s. Connected to it in this case there is a {{nihongo|''Sukibei''|"lattice-windowed wall"}}, a 200 meter wall surrounding the ''honden''.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A similar combination of ''karamon'' and ''Sukibei'' can be seen at the [[Ueno Tōshō-gū]],{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} another [[Edo period]] [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tokyo]] closely associated to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Both structures were built in 1706, and both of them are designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Karamon 2.jpg|front view of the ''karamon'' File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 2.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the outside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 4.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the inside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 6.jpg|''honden'' seen through the ''Sukibei'' File:Nezu-jinja-4.jpg|[[Mikoshi]] File:AzaleaFestivalNezuJinja.jpg|[[Rhododendron]] == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} The entrances are at a 5 minute walk from [[Nezu Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Chiyoda Line|Chiyoda Line]] (9 minute from [[Sendagi Station]] in the same line) and at a 6 minute walk from [[Todaimae Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Namboku Line]].{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category|Nezu-jinja|Nezu Shrine}} *[http://www.nedujinja.or.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Bunkyō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nezu Shrine
根津神社 | image = Nezu Shrine 2010.jpg | caption = The Nezu Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|43|13|N|139|45|39|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Yamato Takeru]] | established = 1705 | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-28-9 Nezu, [[Bunkyō]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 113-0031 | website = {{URL|http://www.nedujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nezu Shrine'''|根津神社|Nezu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Bunkyō]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established in 1705, it is one of the oldest places of worship in the city, and several of the buildings on the shrine grounds have been designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. It was built in the [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] style of [[Shinto architecture]], following the [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū shrine]] in [[Nikkō]]. It is famous{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} for its [[Azalea]] Festival (Tsutsuji Matsuri) which is held on its grounds from early April until early May, and it has been described as "[[Tokyo]]’s most beautiful shrine"{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and as one of the city's "most spectacular spring scenes".{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}.{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == History == According to the legend, the Nezu shrine was founded in Sendagi, just north of the current location, in the 1st century by [[Yamato Takeru]] (also known as Prince Ōsu), the son of [[Emperor Keikō]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Shinto-Shrines-Sacred-Ancient-Religion/dp/0824837134 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |pages=81 |language=English}} The chief deity of the shrine was [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the [[kami]] of the [[sea]] and [[storms]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In 1705{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo's most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} the shrine was relocated to Nezu by [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1646–1709), the fifth ''[[shōgun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa dynasty]], on the occasion of him choosing his successor, [[Tokugawa Ienobu]] (1662–1712).{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In turn, [[Ienobu]] chose it as the guardian deity.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Nothing is left from the structures prior to the relocation,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} but it still is one of the oldest shrines in the city.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} When [[Emperor Meiji]] moved his residence from the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] to the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] in [[Tokyo]] in 1868-1869, he sent envoys to the shrine to have it intercede with the gods on his behalf.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == Architecture == === Torii === There is a multitude of {{nihongo|''[[torii]]''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} surrounding Nezu Shrine. The two main entrances are marked by big red ''torii'' in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A plaque on top of them reads ''根津神社'', the name of the shrine. They are flanked by lanterns. One of the most famous{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} features of the shrine is the path of vermilion torii through the hillside left of the main hall. In the middle of the path there is a viewing platform over a pond of [[koi]], overlooking the main shrine precincts. The subsidiary Otome Inari Shrine is located here.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Another shorter path of torii leads down some stairs from the subsidiary Komagome Shrine to the larger path of torii. File:Nezu jinja - Torii 1.jpg|red ''myōjin torii'' at one of the entrances File:Nezu jinja - Torii 4.jpg|a stone ''myōjin torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 3.jpg|entrance to the path of ''torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 2.jpg|a shorter ''torii'' path === Rōmon === A two-storied {{nihongo|''[[rōmon]]''|楼門||tower gate}} is a type of [[Mon (architecture)|mon]] characterized by an inaccessible upper floor. Originally an element of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], this type of mon is also commonly found in [[Shinto shrine]]s. The one at Nezu shrine is located between the ''honden'' and a little bridge, which makes it a popular attraction of the shrine.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A placque on top of the gate reads {{nihongo|''Nezu-jinja''|根津神社}}. It is guarded by two [[Zuijin|zuishin]],{{cite web |url=http://www.visiting-japan.com/en/articles/tokyo/e13bu-nezu-jinja.htm |title=Nezu Shrine, Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Visiting Japan |access-date=2017-12-24 |quote=}} statues of [[kami]] warrior-guardians depicted holding bows and arrows. It was built in 1706,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and it is designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Romon 2.jpg|front view of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 3.jpg|placque with the name of the shrine File:Nezu jinja - Romon 1.jpg|detail of one side of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 5.jpg|one of the two ''[[Zuijin|zuishin]]'' statue === Honden === The main building of Nezu shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof. The whole structure dates from 1706. All three parts of the building (''honden'', ''haiden'' and ''heiden'') are separately designated as an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Honden 12.jpg|front view of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 4.jpg|detail of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 11.jpg|interior of the ''honden'' and ''haiden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 1.jpg|the ''honden'' seen from the viewing platform === Karamon and Sukibei walls === A {{nihongo|''[[karamon]]''|唐門|"chinese gate"}} is a type of ''mon'' found in [[Japanese castle]]s, [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine]]s. Connected to it in this case there is a {{nihongo|''Sukibei''|"lattice-windowed wall"}}, a 200 meter wall surrounding the ''honden''.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A similar combination of ''karamon'' and ''Sukibei'' can be seen at the [[Ueno Tōshō-gū]],{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} another [[Edo period]] [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tokyo]] closely associated to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Both structures were built in 1706, and both of them are designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Karamon 2.jpg|front view of the ''karamon'' File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 2.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the outside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 4.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the inside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 6.jpg|''honden'' seen through the ''Sukibei'' File:Nezu-jinja-4.jpg|[[Mikoshi]] File:AzaleaFestivalNezuJinja.jpg|[[Rhododendron]] == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} The entrances are at a 5 minute walk from [[Nezu Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Chiyoda Line|Chiyoda Line]] (9 minute from [[Sendagi Station]] in the same line) and at a 6 minute walk from [[Todaimae Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Namboku Line]].{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category|Nezu-jinja|Nezu Shrine}} *[http://www.nedujinja.or.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Established in 1705, it is one of the oldest places of worship in the city, and several of the buildings on the shrine grounds have been designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. It was built in the [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] style of [[Shinto architecture]], following the [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū shrine]] in [[Nikkō]]. It is famous{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} for its [[Azalea]] Festival (Tsutsuji Matsuri) which is held on its grounds from early April until early May, and it has been described as "[[Tokyo]]’s most beautiful shrine"{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and as one of the city's "most spectacular spring scenes".{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}.{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == History == According to the legend, the Nezu shrine was founded in Sendagi, just north of the current location, in the 1st century by [[Yamato Takeru]] (also known as Prince Ōsu), the son of [[Emperor Keikō]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Shinto-Shrines-Sacred-Ancient-Religion/dp/0824837134 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the [[kami]] of the [[sea]] and [[storms]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In 1705{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo's most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} the shrine was relocated to Nezu by [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1646–1709), the fifth ''[[shōgun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa dynasty]], on the occasion of him choosing his successor, [[Tokugawa Ienobu]] (1662–1712).{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In turn, [[Ienobu]] chose it as the guardian deity.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Nothing is left from the structures prior to the relocation,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} but it still is one of the oldest shrines in the city.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} When [[Emperor Meiji]] moved his residence from the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] to the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] in [[Tokyo]] in 1868-1869, he sent envoys to the shrine to have it intercede with the gods on his behalf.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == Architecture == === Torii === There is a multitude of {{nihongo|''[[torii]]''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} surrounding Nezu Shrine. The two main entrances are marked by big red ''torii'' in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A plaque on top of them reads ''根津神社'', the name of the shrine. They are flanked by lanterns. One of the most famous{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} features of the shrine is the path of vermilion torii through the hillside left of the main hall. In the middle of the path there is a viewing platform over a pond of [[koi]], overlooking the main shrine precincts. The subsidiary Otome Inari Shrine is located here.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Another shorter path of torii leads down some stairs from the subsidiary Komagome Shrine to the larger path of torii. File:Nezu jinja - Torii 1.jpg|red ''myōjin torii'' at one of the entrances File:Nezu jinja - Torii 4.jpg|a stone ''myōjin torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 3.jpg|entrance to the path of ''torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 2.jpg|a shorter ''torii'' path === Rōmon === A two-storied {{nihongo|''[[rōmon]]''|楼門||tower gate}} is a type of [[Mon (architecture)|mon]] characterized by an inaccessible upper floor. Originally an element of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], this type of mon is also commonly found in [[Shinto shrine]]s. The one at Nezu shrine is located between the ''honden'' and a little bridge, which makes it a popular attraction of the shrine.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A placque on top of the gate reads {{nihongo|''Nezu-jinja''|根津神社}}. It is guarded by two [[Zuijin|zuishin]],{{cite web |url=http://www.visiting-japan.com/en/articles/tokyo/e13bu-nezu-jinja.htm |title=Nezu Shrine, Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Visiting Japan |access-date=2017-12-24 |quote=}} statues of [[kami]] warrior-guardians depicted holding bows and arrows. It was built in 1706,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and it is designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Romon 2.jpg|front view of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 3.jpg|placque with the name of the shrine File:Nezu jinja - Romon 1.jpg|detail of one side of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 5.jpg|one of the two ''[[Zuijin|zuishin]]'' statue === Honden === The main building of Nezu shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof. The whole structure dates from 1706. All three parts of the building (''honden'', ''haiden'' and ''heiden'') are separately designated as an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Honden 12.jpg|front view of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 4.jpg|detail of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 11.jpg|interior of the ''honden'' and ''haiden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 1.jpg|the ''honden'' seen from the viewing platform === Karamon and Sukibei walls === A {{nihongo|''[[karamon]]''|唐門|"chinese gate"}} is a type of ''mon'' found in [[Japanese castle]]s, [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine]]s. Connected to it in this case there is a {{nihongo|''Sukibei''|"lattice-windowed wall"}}, a 200 meter wall surrounding the ''honden''.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A similar combination of ''karamon'' and ''Sukibei'' can be seen at the [[Ueno Tōshō-gū]],{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} another [[Edo period]] [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tokyo]] closely associated to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Both structures were built in 1706, and both of them are designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Karamon 2.jpg|front view of the ''karamon'' File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 2.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the outside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 4.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the inside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 6.jpg|''honden'' seen through the ''Sukibei'' File:Nezu-jinja-4.jpg|[[Mikoshi]] File:AzaleaFestivalNezuJinja.jpg|[[Rhododendron]] == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} The entrances are at a 5 minute walk from [[Nezu Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Chiyoda Line|Chiyoda Line]] (9 minute from [[Sendagi Station]] in the same line) and at a 6 minute walk from [[Todaimae Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Namboku Line]].{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nezu Shrine
根津神社 | image = Nezu Shrine 2010.jpg | caption = The Nezu Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|43|13|N|139|45|39|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Yamato Takeru]] | established = 1705 | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-28-9 Nezu, [[Bunkyō]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 113-0031 | website = {{URL|http://www.nedujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nezu Shrine'''|根津神社|Nezu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Bunkyō]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established in 1705, it is one of the oldest places of worship in the city, and several of the buildings on the shrine grounds have been designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. It was built in the [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] style of [[Shinto architecture]], following the [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū shrine]] in [[Nikkō]]. It is famous{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} for its [[Azalea]] Festival (Tsutsuji Matsuri) which is held on its grounds from early April until early May, and it has been described as "[[Tokyo]]’s most beautiful shrine"{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and as one of the city's "most spectacular spring scenes".{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}.{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == History == According to the legend, the Nezu shrine was founded in Sendagi, just north of the current location, in the 1st century by [[Yamato Takeru]] (also known as Prince Ōsu), the son of [[Emperor Keikō]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Shinto-Shrines-Sacred-Ancient-Religion/dp/0824837134 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |pages=81 |language=English}} The chief deity of the shrine was [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the [[kami]] of the [[sea]] and [[storms]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In 1705{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo's most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} the shrine was relocated to Nezu by [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1646–1709), the fifth ''[[shōgun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa dynasty]], on the occasion of him choosing his successor, [[Tokugawa Ienobu]] (1662–1712).{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In turn, [[Ienobu]] chose it as the guardian deity.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Nothing is left from the structures prior to the relocation,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} but it still is one of the oldest shrines in the city.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} When [[Emperor Meiji]] moved his residence from the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] to the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] in [[Tokyo]] in 1868-1869, he sent envoys to the shrine to have it intercede with the gods on his behalf.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == Architecture == === Torii === There is a multitude of {{nihongo|''[[torii]]''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} surrounding Nezu Shrine. The two main entrances are marked by big red ''torii'' in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A plaque on top of them reads ''根津神社'', the name of the shrine. They are flanked by lanterns. One of the most famous{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} features of the shrine is the path of vermilion torii through the hillside left of the main hall. In the middle of the path there is a viewing platform over a pond of [[koi]], overlooking the main shrine precincts. The subsidiary Otome Inari Shrine is located here.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Another shorter path of torii leads down some stairs from the subsidiary Komagome Shrine to the larger path of torii. File:Nezu jinja - Torii 1.jpg|red ''myōjin torii'' at one of the entrances File:Nezu jinja - Torii 4.jpg|a stone ''myōjin torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 3.jpg|entrance to the path of ''torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 2.jpg|a shorter ''torii'' path === Rōmon === A two-storied {{nihongo|''[[rōmon]]''|楼門||tower gate}} is a type of [[Mon (architecture)|mon]] characterized by an inaccessible upper floor. Originally an element of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], this type of mon is also commonly found in [[Shinto shrine]]s. The one at Nezu shrine is located between the ''honden'' and a little bridge, which makes it a popular attraction of the shrine.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A placque on top of the gate reads {{nihongo|''Nezu-jinja''|根津神社}}. It is guarded by two [[Zuijin|zuishin]],{{cite web |url=http://www.visiting-japan.com/en/articles/tokyo/e13bu-nezu-jinja.htm |title=Nezu Shrine, Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Visiting Japan |access-date=2017-12-24 |quote=}} statues of [[kami]] warrior-guardians depicted holding bows and arrows. It was built in 1706,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and it is designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Romon 2.jpg|front view of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 3.jpg|placque with the name of the shrine File:Nezu jinja - Romon 1.jpg|detail of one side of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 5.jpg|one of the two ''[[Zuijin|zuishin]]'' statue === Honden === The main building of Nezu shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof. The whole structure dates from 1706. All three parts of the building (''honden'', ''haiden'' and ''heiden'') are separately designated as an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Honden 12.jpg|front view of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 4.jpg|detail of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 11.jpg|interior of the ''honden'' and ''haiden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 1.jpg|the ''honden'' seen from the viewing platform === Karamon and Sukibei walls === A {{nihongo|''[[karamon]]''|唐門|"chinese gate"}} is a type of ''mon'' found in [[Japanese castle]]s, [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine]]s. Connected to it in this case there is a {{nihongo|''Sukibei''|"lattice-windowed wall"}}, a 200 meter wall surrounding the ''honden''.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A similar combination of ''karamon'' and ''Sukibei'' can be seen at the [[Ueno Tōshō-gū]],{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} another [[Edo period]] [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tokyo]] closely associated to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Both structures were built in 1706, and both of them are designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Karamon 2.jpg|front view of the ''karamon'' File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 2.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the outside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 4.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the inside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 6.jpg|''honden'' seen through the ''Sukibei'' File:Nezu-jinja-4.jpg|[[Mikoshi]] File:AzaleaFestivalNezuJinja.jpg|[[Rhododendron]] == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} The entrances are at a 5 minute walk from [[Nezu Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Chiyoda Line|Chiyoda Line]] (9 minute from [[Sendagi Station]] in the same line) and at a 6 minute walk from [[Todaimae Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Namboku Line]].{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nezu Shrine
根津神社 | image = Nezu Shrine 2010.jpg | caption = The Nezu Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|43|13|N|139|45|39|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Yamato Takeru]] | established = 1705 | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-28-9 Nezu, [[Bunkyō]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 113-0031 | website = {{URL|http://www.nedujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nezu Shrine'''|根津神社|Nezu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Bunkyō]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established in 1705, it is one of the oldest places of worship in the city, and several of the buildings on the shrine grounds have been designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. It was built in the [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] style of [[Shinto architecture]], following the [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū shrine]] in [[Nikkō]]. It is famous{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} for its [[Azalea]] Festival (Tsutsuji Matsuri) which is held on its grounds from early April until early May, and it has been described as "[[Tokyo]]’s most beautiful shrine"{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and as one of the city's "most spectacular spring scenes".{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}.{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == History == According to the legend, the Nezu shrine was founded in Sendagi, just north of the current location, in the 1st century by [[Yamato Takeru]] (also known as Prince Ōsu), the son of [[Emperor Keikō]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Shinto-Shrines-Sacred-Ancient-Religion/dp/0824837134 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |pages=81 |language=English}} The chief deity of the shrine was [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]], the [[kami]] of the [[sea]] and [[storms]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In 1705{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo's most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} the shrine was relocated to Nezu by [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1646–1709), the fifth ''[[shōgun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa dynasty]], on the occasion of him choosing his successor, [[Tokugawa Ienobu]] (1662–1712).{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In turn, [[Ienobu]] chose it as the guardian deity.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Nothing is left from the structures prior to the relocation,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} but it still is one of the oldest shrines in the city.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} When [[Emperor Meiji]] moved his residence from the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] to the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] in [[Tokyo]] in 1868-1869, he sent envoys to the shrine to have it intercede with the gods on his behalf.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == Architecture == === Torii === There is a multitude of {{nihongo|''[[torii]]''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} surrounding Nezu Shrine. The two main entrances are marked by big red ''torii'' in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A plaque on top of them reads ''根津神社'', the name of the shrine. They are flanked by lanterns. One of the most famous{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} features of the shrine is the path of vermilion torii through the hillside left of the main hall. In the middle of the path there is a viewing platform over a pond of [[koi]], overlooking the main shrine precincts. The subsidiary Otome Inari Shrine is located here.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Another shorter path of torii leads down some stairs from the subsidiary Komagome Shrine to the larger path of torii. File:Nezu jinja - Torii 1.jpg|red ''myōjin torii'' at one of the entrances File:Nezu jinja - Torii 4.jpg|a stone ''myōjin torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 3.jpg|entrance to the path of ''torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 2.jpg|a shorter ''torii'' path === Rōmon === A two-storied {{nihongo|''[[rōmon]]''|楼門||tower gate}} is a type of [[Mon (architecture)|mon]] characterized by an inaccessible upper floor. Originally an element of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], this type of mon is also commonly found in [[Shinto shrine]]s. The one at Nezu shrine is located between the ''honden'' and a little bridge, which makes it a popular attraction of the shrine.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A placque on top of the gate reads {{nihongo|''Nezu-jinja''|根津神社}}. It is guarded by two [[Zuijin|zuishin]],{{cite web |url=http://www.visiting-japan.com/en/articles/tokyo/e13bu-nezu-jinja.htm |title=Nezu Shrine, Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Visiting Japan |access-date=2017-12-24 |quote=}} statues of [[kami]] warrior-guardians depicted holding bows and arrows. It was built in 1706,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and it is designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Romon 2.jpg|front view of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 3.jpg|placque with the name of the shrine File:Nezu jinja - Romon 1.jpg|detail of one side of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 5.jpg|one of the two ''[[Zuijin|zuishin]]'' statue === Honden === The main building of Nezu shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof. The whole structure dates from 1706. All three parts of the building (''honden'', ''haiden'' and ''heiden'') are separately designated as an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Honden 12.jpg|front view of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 4.jpg|detail of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 11.jpg|interior of the ''honden'' and ''haiden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 1.jpg|the ''honden'' seen from the viewing platform === Karamon and Sukibei walls === A {{nihongo|''[[karamon]]''|唐門|"chinese gate"}} is a type of ''mon'' found in [[Japanese castle]]s, [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine]]s. Connected to it in this case there is a {{nihongo|''Sukibei''|"lattice-windowed wall"}}, a 200 meter wall surrounding the ''honden''.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A similar combination of ''karamon'' and ''Sukibei'' can be seen at the [[Ueno Tōshō-gū]],{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} another [[Edo period]] [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tokyo]] closely associated to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Both structures were built in 1706, and both of them are designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Karamon 2.jpg|front view of the ''karamon'' File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 2.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the outside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 4.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the inside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 6.jpg|''honden'' seen through the ''Sukibei'' File:Nezu-jinja-4.jpg|[[Mikoshi]] File:AzaleaFestivalNezuJinja.jpg|[[Rhododendron]] == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} The entrances are at a 5 minute walk from [[Nezu Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Chiyoda Line|Chiyoda Line]] (9 minute from [[Sendagi Station]] in the same line) and at a 6 minute walk from [[Todaimae Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Namboku Line]].{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category|Nezu-jinja|Nezu Shrine}} *[http://www.nedujinja.or.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and in Bunkyō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:kk]] 根津神社 | image = Nezu Shrine 2010.jpg | caption = The Nezu Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|43|13|N|139|45|39|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Yamato Takeru]] | established = 1705 | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-28-9 Nezu, [[Bunkyō]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 113-0031 | website = {{URL|http://www.nedujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nezu Shrine'''|根津神社|Nezu-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Bunkyō]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established in 1705, it is one of the oldest places of worship in the city, and several of the buildings on the shrine grounds have been designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. It was built in the [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] style of [[Shinto architecture]], following the [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū shrine]] in [[Nikkō]]. It is famous{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} for its [[Azalea]] Festival (Tsutsuji Matsuri) which is held on its grounds from early April until early May, and it has been described as "[[Tokyo]]’s most beautiful shrine"{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and as one of the city's "most spectacular spring scenes".{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}.{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == History == According to the legend, the Nezu shrine was founded in Sendagi, just north of the current location, in the 1st century by [[Yamato Takeru]] (also known as Prince Ōsu), the son of [[Emperor Keikō]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Shinto-Shrines-Sacred-Ancient-Religion/dp/0824837134 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In 1705{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo's most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} the shrine was relocated to Nezu by [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] (1646–1709), the fifth ''[[shōgun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa dynasty]], on the occasion of him choosing his successor, [[Tokugawa Ienobu]] (1662–1712).{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} In turn, [[Ienobu]] chose it as the guardian deity.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Nothing is left from the structures prior to the relocation,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} but it still is one of the oldest shrines in the city.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} When [[Emperor Meiji]] moved his residence from the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] to the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] in [[Tokyo]] in 1868-1869, he sent envoys to the shrine to have it intercede with the gods on his behalf.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} == Architecture == === Torii === There is a multitude of {{nihongo|''[[torii]]''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} surrounding Nezu Shrine. The two main entrances are marked by big red ''torii'' in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A plaque on top of them reads ''根津神社'', the name of the shrine. They are flanked by lanterns. One of the most famous{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} features of the shrine is the path of vermilion torii through the hillside left of the main hall. In the middle of the path there is a viewing platform over a pond of [[koi]], overlooking the main shrine precincts. The subsidiary Otome Inari Shrine is located here.{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} Another shorter path of torii leads down some stairs from the subsidiary Komagome Shrine to the larger path of torii. File:Nezu jinja - Torii 1.jpg|red ''myōjin torii'' at one of the entrances File:Nezu jinja - Torii 4.jpg|a stone ''myōjin torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 3.jpg|entrance to the path of ''torii'' File:Nezu jinja - Torii 2.jpg|a shorter ''torii'' path === Rōmon === A two-storied {{nihongo|''[[rōmon]]''|楼門||tower gate}} is a type of [[Mon (architecture)|mon]] characterized by an inaccessible upper floor. Originally an element of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], this type of mon is also commonly found in [[Shinto shrine]]s. The one at Nezu shrine is located between the ''honden'' and a little bridge, which makes it a popular attraction of the shrine.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A placque on top of the gate reads {{nihongo|''Nezu-jinja''|根津神社}}. It is guarded by two [[Zuijin|zuishin]],{{cite web |url=http://www.visiting-japan.com/en/articles/tokyo/e13bu-nezu-jinja.htm |title=Nezu Shrine, Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Visiting Japan |access-date=2017-12-24 |quote=}} statues of [[kami]] warrior-guardians depicted holding bows and arrows. It was built in 1706,{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} and it is designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Romon 2.jpg|front view of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 3.jpg|placque with the name of the shrine File:Nezu jinja - Romon 1.jpg|detail of one side of the ''rōmon'' File:Nezu jinja - Romon 5.jpg|one of the two ''[[Zuijin|zuishin]]'' statue === Honden === The main building of Nezu shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof. The whole structure dates from 1706. All three parts of the building (''honden'', ''haiden'' and ''heiden'') are separately designated as an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Honden 12.jpg|front view of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 4.jpg|detail of the ''honden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 11.jpg|interior of the ''honden'' and ''haiden'' File:Nezu jinja - Honden 1.jpg|the ''honden'' seen from the viewing platform === Karamon and Sukibei walls === A {{nihongo|''[[karamon]]''|唐門|"chinese gate"}} is a type of ''mon'' found in [[Japanese castle]]s, [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] and [[Shinto shrine]]s. Connected to it in this case there is a {{nihongo|''Sukibei''|"lattice-windowed wall"}}, a 200 meter wall surrounding the ''honden''.{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} A similar combination of ''karamon'' and ''Sukibei'' can be seen at the [[Ueno Tōshō-gū]],{{cite web |url=https://old-tokyo.info/nezu-shrine/ |title=Nezu Shrine: Tokyo’s most beautiful shinto shrine (with azaleas) |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Exploring Old Tokyo |access-date=2017-12-09 |quote=}} another [[Edo period]] [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tokyo]] closely associated to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Both structures were built in 1706, and both of them are designated as [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]].{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} File:Nezu jinja - Karamon 2.jpg|front view of the ''karamon'' File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 2.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the outside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 4.jpg|the ''Sukibei'' senn from the inside File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 6.jpg|''honden'' seen through the ''Sukibei'' File:Nezu-jinja-4.jpg|[[Mikoshi]] File:AzaleaFestivalNezuJinja.jpg|[[Rhododendron]] == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts.{{cite web |url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/nezujinja.html |title=Nezu-jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} The entrances are at a 5 minute walk from [[Nezu Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Chiyoda Line|Chiyoda Line]] (9 minute from [[Sendagi Station]] in the same line) and at a 6 minute walk from [[Todaimae Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Namboku Line]].{{cite web |url=https://www.japanvisitor.com/japan-temples-shrines/nezu-shrine-tokyo |title=Nezu Shrine Tokyo |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Japan Visitor |access-date=2017-12-12 |quote=}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nezu Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nezu
